giving_tranches.source: [July 2020 essay ('116 Organizations Driving Change')](https://yieldgiving.com/essays): $1.68B; series recapped in [CNBC's year-end 2025 accounting](https://www.cnbc.com/2025/12/13/mackenzie-scott-revealed-her-total-charitable-donations-for-2025.html)

[December 2020 essay ('384 Ways to Help')](https://yieldgiving.com/essays): $4.16B to 384 organizations; recapped in [CNBC 2025-12-13](https://www.cnbc.com/2025/12/13/mackenzie-scott-revealed-her-total-charitable-donations-for-2025.html)

[June 2021 essay ('Seeding by Ceding')](https://yieldgiving.com/essays): $2.74B to 286 organizations; recapped in [CNBC 2025-12-13](https://www.cnbc.com/2025/12/13/mackenzie-scott-revealed-her-total-charitable-donations-for-2025.html)

[March 2022 essay ('Helping Any of Us Can Help Us All')](https://yieldgiving.com/essays): $3.86B to 465 organizations (gifts since June 2021; booked at the 2022 price level — conservative); recapped in [CNBC 2025-12-13](https://www.cnbc.com/2025/12/13/mackenzie-scott-revealed-her-total-charitable-donations-for-2025.html)

qaly_per_death_averted.source: Populations in the causal mortality studies are low-income adults roughly age 50-64. Remaining life expectancy ~26y is calibrated to [Chetty et al. (2016, JAMA)](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jama/fullarticle/2513561): bottom-income-quartile life expectancy at 40 is ~78 (men) to ~83 (women), implying mid-20s remaining years around age 55. Utility ~0.78 is calibrated to US EQ-5D population norms (e.g., [Jiang et al. 2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11136-020-02650-y), ages 55-64 ~0.80 overall, lower at low income). Discounted at 3% with a half-cycle correction.

vqaly_usd.source: [HHS ASPE Standard Values for Regulatory Analysis 2026](https://aspe.hhs.gov/sites/default/files/documents/2d83af5823915d81871334ee08ad03d9/Standard-RIA-Values-2026.pdf), Table 3: VQALY for 2026 impacts at a 3% discount rate, low/central/high $339k / $726k / $1,105k in constant 2025 dollars. Inflated to the May 2026 price level with [CPI-U](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCNS) 335.123 / 321.943 (2025 annual average) = 1.0409: $353k / $756k / $1,150k. One series, one price level, denominated at the 3% reference rate; at other rates the model rescales by the adult PV-QALY ratio (vqaly_adult below), which reproduces HHS Table 2's discounted PV QALYs (19.5 at 3%, 11.7 at 7%) to within 0.1%, keeping the benefit/cost ratio consistent with the discount slider.

frontier_cost_per_qaly_usd.source: Derived from [GiveWell's current impact estimates](https://www.givewell.org/impact-estimates) of cost per life saved, 2022-2024 program averages: ~$4,000 (Malaria Consortium seasonal chemoprevention) to ~$5,500 (AMF nets), figures GiveWell rounds up to the nearest $500. Treating those as ~2023 dollars and inflating by [CPI-U](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCNS) 335.123/304.702 = 1.10 gives ~$4,400-$6,050 per life. A child death averted at ~age 1 with ~65 remaining life-years, discounted at 3% with half-cycle correction (annuity ~28.9) and utility ~0.87, is ~25.1 discounted QALYs, giving endpoints of ~$175-$241 per QALY-equivalent; the loguniform {150, 260} widens that for conversion uncertainty (utility, remaining years, price level). An earlier revision also used a 2015 AMF figure ($3,340/life, itself already a discounted-QALY-era calculation, in ~2015 dollars); it is retired here in favor of GiveWell's current program averages.

realization_factor.source: AI-PROPOSED, AUTHOR-REVIEWED PRIOR (no literature estimate exists for this quantity). Downweight for transport/overhead/funging; upweight allowed past 1.0 because [Center for Effective Philanthropy (2023)](https://cep.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/BigGiftsStudy_Report_Y2_FNL.pdf) finds large unrestricted gifts materially expand recipient capacity/reach. Stress-test with the interactive's realization slider (it moves the MODE of this triangle within [0.55, 1.10]).

strong_quasi.design: Credible quasi-experiment (DD/IV) with admin data and checks

moderate_quasi.design: Quasi-experiment with notable concerns or a contested literature

projection.design: Model/IAM projection; no direct causal estimate for this lever

assumption.design: No causal health evidence; skeptical expert prior

health_coverage.label: Health - insurance & access
health_coverage.identification: DD across staggered state Medicaid expansions with linked coverage data (Sommers 2017; Miller, Johnson & Wherry 2021 corroborate with linked administrative mortality for the ACA expansion). Strong; the Oregon HIE RCT (null on 2-yr physical health measures) tempers it, hence not the top tier.
health_coverage.source: [Sommers (2017), American Journal of Health Economics 3(3)](https://www.journals.uchicago.edu/doi/10.1162/ajhe_a_00080) ([open copy](https://dash.harvard.edu/server/api/core/bitstreams/7312037d-ed01-6bd4-e053-0100007fdf3b/content)): cost per life saved of $327k-$867k "in 2007 dollars" (p.30), from NNT x [$778 x (1+DWL) + $3,156 x DWL] with NNT 239-316 (one death prevented per year per 239-316 adults covered), OHIE spending effects, and deadweight loss 15-50%. Inflated with [FRED CPI-U (CPIAUCNS)](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCNS) from the 2007 annual average 207.342 to May 2026 335.123 (x1.616): $529k-$1.40M in 2026 dollars. Mortality-effect corroboration: [Miller, Johnson & Wherry (2021), QJE 136(3)](https://www.nber.org/papers/w26081) (0.132 pp annual mortality decline, 9.4%).

health_chc.label: Health - community health centers
health_chc.identification: Event-study/DD on the 1960s-70s CHC rollout (Bailey & Goodman-Bacon 2015). Credible design; spatial-spillover concerns in later re-analyses. (Transport to today's CHCs is a realization matter, not identification.)
health_chc.source: [Bailey & Goodman-Bacon (2015), AER 105(3)](https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/aer.20120070) ([full text](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4436657/)) report "a cost-per-year-of-life ratio of approximately $54,000" for CHCs in 2012 dollars (Medicare comparison: $161,373-$459,000 per life-year, i.e. CHCs at 1/8 to 1/3 the cost). Inflated with [FRED CPI-U](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCNS) from the 2012 annual average 229.594 to May 2026 335.123 (x1.460): ~$79k per life-year; the loguniform $55k-$115k places ~±45% around that point estimate. The model divides by the utility-weight draw to convert life-years to QALYs (the prior revision skipped that conversion and used an invented Medicare prior; both fixed here).

health_mental.label: Health - mental & behavioral
health_mental.identification: ICERs come from RCTs of collaborative care for depression (high internal validity). Attribution of Scott's grants to this exact model is a realization matter.
health_mental.source: Anchors are 2000s-era nominal ICERs and are inflated here. The [Community Guide review](https://www.thecommunityguide.org/findings/mental-health-and-mental-illness-collaborative-care-management-depressive-disorders.html) reports the Gilbody-era $17k-$39k/QALY range in 2008 dollars, which [CPI-U](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCNS) 335.123/215.303 = 1.56 inflates to ~$26k-$61k; the systematic review at [van Steenbergen-Weijenburg et al. (2010), BMC Health Services Research 10:19](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/1472-6963-10-19) reports study ICERs of ~$21.5k-$49.5k without year normalization (similar-era dollars, similar inflated magnitude; an earlier revision misattributed this link to "Gilbody et al. 2006" and pooled the two reviews' endpoints as one range). Together the inflated cost-effective case sits around $26k-$77k. The upper tail extends toward [Grochtdreis et al. (2015)](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0123078), whose healthcare-perspective ICERs reach $153,299 in 2012 international dollars (not CPI-converted here; international dollars are not US CPI dollars). The 5th/95th prior {25k, 150k} brackets the inflated evidence.

econ_cash.label: Economic security - cash & financial
econ_cash.identification: Mixed by target population, replicated across developed countries. Adult-targeted cash is credibly ~null on health (Swedish lottery randomization; two US cash RCTs; Swedish lottery wealth buys sustained life satisfaction but significantly smaller mental-health effects). Child-targeted cash shows replicated positive effects in strong quasi-experimental designs in the US (Mothers' Pensions, EITC, casino dividends) and Canada (child-benefit expansions). strong_quasi reflects that the positive anchors are QEDs coexisting with RCT-grade adult nulls.
econ_cash.source: [Cesarini et al. (2016, QJE)](https://academic.oup.com/qje/article/131/2/687/2606947) lottery study finds ~zero causal effect of income on adult mortality in a rich country; the largest US guaranteed-income RCT ([Miller et al. 2024, NBER w32711](https://www.nber.org/papers/w32711): $1,000/month for 3 years) found no physical-health effects and fading first-year stress/mental-health gains; a one-time $500-$2,000 UCT RCT with 5,243 Americans in poverty ([Jaroszewicz et al. 2024](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4154000)) found no improvement on any pre-specified financial, psychological, or health outcome. Child-targeted cash shows real effects in strong quasi-experimental designs, anchoring the low end: [Aizer, Eli, Ferrie & Lleras-Muney (2016, AER)](https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/aer.20140529) Mothers' Pensions ($10-30/month, typically 3 years: ~$360-1,080 nominal 1910s-30s ~ $7k-20k in 2026$ per family via CPI x~18.6) raised accepted applicants' sons' longevity ~1 year (1.5 for the poorest; daughters unobserved) — ~$7k-20k per undiscounted life-year, ~$50k-115k per discounted QALY (3%, ~65-year lag, 0.78 utility); [Hoynes, Miller & Simon (2015, AEJ:Policy)](https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/pol.20120179) $1,000 of EITC (treatment-on-treated) cuts low-birth-weight incidence 2-3% (6.7-10.8% for single low-education mothers); [Great Smoky Mountains casino dividends](https://www.nber.org/papers/w24337) (~$6k/year/household) cut child behavioral problems ~40%, with depression/anxiety reductions persisting to age 30; [Milligan & Stabile (2011, AEJ:Policy)](https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/pol.3.3.175) Canadian child-benefit expansions (province x time x family-type variation) improve child test scores, child mental health, and maternal health. On the adult quality-of-life channel, [Lindqvist, Ostling & Cesarini (2020, REStud)](https://academic.oup.com/restud/article/87/6/2703/5734654) find Swedish lottery wealth yields sustained life-satisfaction gains but significantly smaller happiness and mental-health effects. Broader [EITC health evidence](https://academyhealth.org/sites/default/files/Updated.EITC%20Rapid%20Evidence%20Review%20FINAL.pdf) beyond these designs stays mixed and confounded. Prior US $/QALY estimates exist and run far lower at face value: [Muennig et al. (2016, AJPM)](https://www.ajpmonline.org/article/S0749-3797(16)30249-5/abstract) put state EITC supplements at $7,786/QALY (95% CI $4,100-$13,400) via an interrupted time series on BRFSS self-reported health, and a [2024 VA analysis](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11539017/) put temporary financial assistance at $22,676/QALY. Those designs are what the credibility axis discounts; this prior's low end deliberately starts at the hard-endpoint Aizer anchor instead, so a reader who takes the ITS as causal should treat this range as too conservative. The lognormal $50k-$1.5M (2026$) spans child-targeted-QED optimism to adult-null pessimism; most cash value remains non-health consumption and wellbeing, which QALYs do not capture.

econ_food.label: Economic security - food security
econ_food.identification: SNAP/health links are largely associational with endogenous take-up (sicker or poorer households select in/out); strong confounding risk. The cost-per-QALY assumes the effect is real; credibility says it may not be causal.
econ_food.source: Judgmental 2026-dollar range CONDITIONAL on the association being causal, anchored to (not derived from) [CBPP syntheses](https://www.cbpp.org/research/food-assistance/snap-is-linked-with-improved-health-outcomes-and-lower-health-care-costs) and food-is-medicine RCTs: SNAP linked to ~$1,400/yr lower health costs and fewer hospitalizations among food-insecure seniors. Causal doubt is carried by the observational credibility tier, NOT by inflating this range (avoids double-counting).

econ_housing.label: Economic security - housing & homelessness
econ_housing.identification: NASEM (2018) judged the PSH health evidence MIXED: few RCTs, mostly pre/post and quasi-experimental, contested cost-savings. Real signal for high-need subgroups, weak identification overall.
econ_housing.source: Point anchor: [Holtgrave et al. (2013), AIDS and Behavior 17(5)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10461-012-0204-3) report $62,493/QALY for the Housing & Health intervention — an HIV-positive, unstably-housed cohort (2004-2008 study), NOT general chronic homelessness. The team's [underlying cost analysis](https://shnny.org/uploads/Cost_and_Threshold_Analysis_of_Housing_as_an_HIV_Prevention.pdf) is stated in 2005 dollars; inflating by [CPI-U](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCNS) 335.123/195.267 = 1.716 gives ~$107k/QALY (an earlier revision assumed 2008 dollars, ~$97k). The {30k, 220k} prior brackets that with room for cheaper high-need PSH cases (frequent cost offsets) and costlier general populations. [NASEM (2018)](https://nap.nationalacademies.org/read/25133/chapter/6) is cited for the mixed-evidence assessment only.

econ_workforce.label: Economic security - workforce & mobility
econ_workforce.identification: Health effect inferred from the income-health gradient with a causal discount; little direct experimental evidence that workforce grants move health. Consistent with the credible ~null income-mortality result.
econ_workforce.source: Judgmental 2026-dollar range CONDITIONAL on workforce/earnings gains translating to health via the income-health gradient. The causal doubt (~null per [Cesarini et al. 2016](https://academic.oup.com/qje/article/131/2/687/2606947)) is carried by the observational credibility tier, not this range. Most value is economic, not health.

education.label: Education (HBCUs, comm. college, scholarships)
education.identification: Compulsory-schooling IV/quasi-experiments (Lleras-Muney; Lager & Torssander; Dutch reform) are credible but the literature is genuinely contested — Clark & Royer (2013) find ~null mortality effects. Wide.
education.source: Judgmental 2026-dollar range anchored to the contested education -> mortality literature: [van Kippersluis, O'Donnell & van Doorslaer (2011)](https://jhr.uwpress.org/content/46/4/695.short), Journal of Human Resources 46(4) (+1 yr schooling -> ~6% lower mortality); [Lleras-Muney (2005)](https://academic.oup.com/restud/article-abstract/72/1/189/1581055); vs [Clark & Royer (2013)](https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/aer.103.6.2087) ~null. Long lag; large non-health (earnings) value excluded.

equity_justice.label: Equity & justice
equity_justice.identification: No credible causal estimate links Scott's equity/justice grants to QALYs. Plausible indirect pathways (e.g., incarceration-mortality) exist but attribution is speculative; value is overwhelmingly non-health.
equity_justice.source: SKEPTICAL AUTHOR PRIOR (2026 dollars); no anchoring study exists. Cause mix informed by the [HBS analysis of Yield Giving recipients](https://www.library.hbs.edu/working-knowledge/mackenzie-scotts-15-billion-pledge-what-the-data-says-about-her-epic-giving): racial equity, criminal-justice reform, immigration, gender. Health pathway is weak/indirect; most value is non-health (rights, fairness, opportunity).

civic_democracy.label: Civic / democracy / philanthropy infra
civic_democracy.identification: No causal health evidence; value is civic/institutional.
civic_democracy.source: SKEPTICAL AUTHOR PRIOR (2026 dollars); no anchoring study exists. Negligible direct health pathway; value is civic and institutional.

arts_culture.label: Arts & culture
arts_culture.identification: Arts-and-health literature exists (WHO 2019; Bygren et al.) but is observational and about participation programs, not institutional grants. Treated as a skeptical near-zero prior.
arts_culture.source: SKEPTICAL AUTHOR PRIOR (2026 dollars). Arts-health evidence such as the [WHO 2019 scoping review](https://www.who.int/europe/publications/i/item/9789289054553) concerns participation and wellbeing, not institutional grants. Negligible direct health pathway; wellbeing value not captured by QALYs.

environment.label: Environment / climate
environment.identification: Partition of doubts (each in exactly one layer): the global realization factor covers only the GENERIC grant-delivery gap it covers everywhere (overhead, funging, transport to the funded organization); this archetype's wide cost-per-QALY prior covers the PROGRAM-level step from an effective climate organization's dollar to abatement; and the projection tier covers trust in Bressler (2021)'s IAM mortality projection itself.
environment.source: Wide judgmental 2026-dollar range ANCHORED TO, not derived from, [Bressler (2021, Nature Communications)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-021-24487-w) mortality cost of carbon (~4,434 tCO2 per excess death, 2020-2100): no abatement-leverage chain is modeled, so the effective $/QALY is a deliberately wide prior, not a calculation.

other_community.label: Other / general community
other_community.identification: Blended social-services proxy; no single identified estimate.
other_community.source: Blended social-services proxy (judgmental, 2026 dollars) for unclassified giving, with recipient mix informed by the [HBS analysis of Yield Giving recipients](https://www.library.hbs.edu/working-knowledge/mackenzie-scotts-15-billion-pledge-what-the-data-says-about-her-epic-giving).
